schools.summary: Abraham sojourns in Gerar: 'and Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur, and sojourned in Gerar' — Abraham leaving Mamre (where he had dwelt, 13:18; 18:1) and journeying southward, into the region between Kadesh and Shur (toward the Negev and the border of Egypt, the patriarch, a pilgrim and stranger, Hebrews 11:9, moving his tents, perhaps moved by the destruction of Sodom, ch. 19, the region near it now desolate); Abraham 'sojourned in Gerar' (a city of the Philistines, 26:1, ruled by Abimelech, the patriarch dwelling as a stranger among the Philistines, the sojourn that would bring the incident with Abimelech, vv.2-18); Abraham's journeying and sojourning marking his life as a stranger and pilgrim, dwelling in tents, moving from place to place, possessing none of the land (Acts 7:5, the setting for the second sister-incident, the patriarch among a strange people) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham journeyed from thence toward the south country, and dwelled between Kadesh and Shur, and sojourned in Gerar
Abraham's journey to and sojourn in Gerar
the pilgrim patriarch moving his tents, dwelling among the Philistines
the setting for the incident with Abimelech (Hebrews 11:9; Acts 7:5)

schools.summary: Gill and witnesses (a repeated fault of unbelief): 'and Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah' — Abraham again giving out that Sarah was his sister (as in Egypt, 12:13, the same infirmity, the same evil into which he had fallen before, the sad relapse of the patriarch into the same sin of unbelief and the fear of man, v.11, not learning from the first deliverance, 12:17-20, the famed father of faith falling twice into the same fault); 'Abimelech... sent, and took Sarah' (the king of Gerar, supposing her unmarried, taking Sarah, into his house, to be his wife or concubine, the deception again endangering Sarah, now about ninety, yet still fair, or honored as a princess, and the promise, the seed, soon to be born, 17:21; 18:10, the stratagem of unbelief bringing peril, requiring God's intervention, vv.3-7).

Keil and Clarke (the half-truth): the saying a half-truth — Sarah indeed Abraham's half-sister (the daughter of his father, not his mother, v.12), not a direct lie, but an equivocation meant to conceal that she was his wife, and so to deceive, the suppression of the whole truth, the fault of unbelief, trusting his own contrivance rather than God's protection, the same equivocation as before (12:13); in either reading a fault of weak faith that endangered Sarah and the promise; the act divides between a repeated fault/relapse into the same sin and an equivocation literally true.

debates.question: What is Abraham's repeated 'She is my sister'?

sides.position: A repeated fault of unbelief and the fear of man - the same sin as in Egypt (12:13), the patriarch's sad relapse, not learning from the first deliverance
An equivocation literally true (Sarah his half-sister, v.12) - the half-truth meant to deceive, yet not a direct lie

themes: And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, She is my sister: and Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah
Abraham's repeated deception over Sarah (the crux of the relapse)
the same fault of unbelief as in Egypt (12:13) vs an equivocation literally true (his half-sister, v.12)
the weak faith that again endangered Sarah and the promise

schools.summary: God warns Abimelech in a dream: 'but God came to Abimelech in a dream by night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a man's wife' — God revealing himself to the heathen king Abimelech in a dream (the divine intervention to protect Sarah and the promise, God dealing even with a pagan king by revelation, the means by which God warned and restrained Abimelech); God warning Abimelech, 'thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken' (the threat of death for taking another man's wife, the solemn warning of judgment, God's protection of Sarah's honor by threatening the king, the danger Abimelech was in, though innocent in intention, v.5); 'for she is a man's wife' (the ground of the warning that Sarah was a married woman, Abraham's wife, whatever Abimelech had been told, God's regard to the marriage bond and to Sarah, the deception, v.2, exposed by God, the LORD intervening to prevent the wrong and protect the promise) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night, and said to him, Behold, thou art but a dead man, for the woman which thou hast taken; for she is a man's wife
God's warning to Abimelech in a dream
God intervening to protect Sarah's honor and the promise
the deception exposed, the marriage bond regarded

schools.summary: Abimelech had not touched Sarah, and pleads innocence: 'but Abimelech had not come near her: and he said, Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?' — Abimelech not yet having touched Sarah (God having restrained him, v.6, the providence of God preserving Sarah's honor, the king kept from the sin, the protection of the promise, the seed soon to be born, 17:21); Abimelech pleading, 'Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?' (protesting that he, and his nation, had acted innocently, not knowing Sarah was married, the appeal to God's justice, that he would not slay the innocent, the king's conscience clear of intentional wrong, the plea of one who had been deceived); 'wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?' (Abimelech appealing that he and his people were guiltless in this matter, having been deceived, v.5, the protest of integrity, the appeal to the Judge of all the earth, cf. 18:25, not to destroy the innocent, the heathen king's sense of justice) — Gill, JFB, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But Abimelech had not come near her: and he said, Lord, wilt thou slay also a righteous nation?
Abimelech's not having touched Sarah and his plea of innocence
God preserving Sarah's honor, the king kept from the sin
the appeal to God's justice not to slay the innocent (18:25)

schools.summary: Abimelech protests his integrity: 'said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself said, He is my brother: in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this' — Abimelech recounting that both Abraham and Sarah had told him they were brother and sister (the deception having come from both, Sarah complying with Abraham's plan, 12:13; 20:13, the king deceived by their words, his innocence in acting on what he was told); Abimelech protesting 'in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this' (his sincerity of heart, no evil intent, not knowing Sarah was married, and his innocence of hand, not having touched her, v.4, the king's clear conscience in the matter, the protest of one who had acted in good faith, the integrity that God himself acknowledged, v.6); Abimelech's 'integrity of heart and innocency of hands' marking his good faith (he had not intended adultery, having been deceived, the heathen king's conscience, God acknowledging his integrity, v.6, yet having restrained him from the actual sin, the distinction between intentional and unintentional wrong) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Said he not unto me, She is my sister? and she, even she herself said, He is my brother: in the integrity of my heart and innocency of my hands have I done this
Abimelech's protest of his integrity and innocence
the king deceived by both Abraham and Sarah (12:13; 20:13)
his sincerity of heart and clear conscience, which God acknowledged (v.6)

schools.summary: God restrains Abimelech from sin (restraining grace): 'and God said unto him in a dream, Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me: therefore suffered I thee not to touch her' — God acknowledging Abimelech's sincerity (v.5, the king having acted without evil intent, being deceived, God, who knows the heart, confirming his good faith, the divine recognition of the king's integrity in the matter); the great word — 'I also withheld thee from sinning against me' (God himself having restrained Abimelech from committing the sin, touching Sarah, the doctrine of restraining, preventing, grace: God keeping men, even the heathen and the wicked, from sins they would otherwise commit, whether by inward restraint, the Spirit bridling the heart, holding it back, or by his providence, taking away the opportunity, here perhaps by an affliction, v.17, or by some hindrance, God's sovereign hand in preventing sin, the LORD restraining the king to protect Sarah and the promise, the sin would have been 'against me, God,' for all sin, even against man, is ultimately against God, Psalm 51:4); 'therefore suffered I thee not to touch her' (God not permitting Abimelech to touch Sarah, the actual preservation of her honor, the restraining grace effecting the protection, the providence that kept the king from the sin, God's care for Sarah and the covenant-seed) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How did God 'withhold' Abimelech from sinning?

sides.position: By restraining (preventing) grace - God keeps men, even the heathen, from sins they would commit, by inward restraint of the Spirit bridling the heart, or by his providence removing the opportunity (all sin being ultimately against God)

themes: And God said unto him in a dream, Yea, I know that thou didst this in the integrity of thy heart; for I also withheld thee from sinning against me: therefore suffered I thee not to touch her
God restraining Abimelech from sin (the crux of restraining grace)
God keeping men, even the heathen, from sins they would commit (by the Spirit or by providence)
all sin ultimately against God (Psalm 51:4), the LORD protecting Sarah and the promise

schools.summary: Restore Sarah, for Abraham is a prophet: 'now therefore restore the man his wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live: and if thou restore her not, know thou that thou shalt surely die, thou, and all that are thine' — God commanding Abimelech to give Sarah back to Abraham (the requirement to undo the wrong, though unintentional, the restoring of Sarah to her husband, the way of safety for the king); Abraham called 'a prophet' (nabi) — the first occurrence of the word 'prophet' in Scripture (Clarke noting that 'prophet' here does not mean primarily a foreteller of the future, but, the Hebrew nabi, one who intercedes, who speaks to God for men, and from God to men, the prophet as the intercessor, the man near to God whose prayers prevail, Abraham, the friend of God, named a prophet whose intercession would heal, v.17, the office of the prophet as intercessor and revealer; some taking 'prophet' in the fuller sense, one to whom God reveals himself, vv.3-7, and who speaks for God, the title marking Abraham's special relation to God); Abraham's prayer would obtain Abimelech's life and healing (v.17, but if the king restored not Sarah, he and all his would die, the offer of life through the prophet's intercession, and the threat of death for disobedience, the power of the prophet's prayer, the way of life set before the king) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: In what sense is Abraham called 'a prophet' (nabi, the first occurrence)?

sides.position: A prophet in the sense of the intercessor - one who speaks to God for men, whose prayer prevails (and from God to men)
A prophet in the fuller sense - one to whom God reveals himself and who speaks for God

themes: Now therefore restore the man his wife; for he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee, and thou shalt live... thou shalt surely die
the command to restore Sarah, and Abraham named a prophet who shall pray (the crux of 'prophet')
the first occurrence of 'prophet' (nabi) - the intercessor whose prayer prevails vs the revealer who speaks for God
Abraham's prayer would heal Abimelech (v.17), the alternative being death

schools.summary: Abimelech's prompt response: 'therefore Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ears: and the men were sore afraid' — the king, alarmed by the dream (vv.3-7), rising early and telling his servants all that God had said (the prompt, serious response to the divine warning, the king sharing the matter with his court); Abimelech rising early (the urgency and seriousness of his response to God's warning, the king's care to obey and to set things right, the diligence prompted by the fear of God's threat); 'the men were sore afraid' (the servants, hearing of God's warning and the danger, death threatened to the king and all his, v.7, greatly afraid, the fear of God's judgment falling on the household, the dread that the king's act had endangered them all, the awe of the divine warning) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: Therefore Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ears: and the men were sore afraid
Abimelech's prompt response and his servants' fear
the king's serious, diligent response to the divine warning
the court's dread of the judgment the act had threatened

schools.summary: Abimelech rebukes Abraham: 'then Abimelech called Abraham, and said unto him, What hast thou done unto us? and what have I offended thee, that thou hast brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done' — the king reproving Abraham for the deception that had endangered him and his kingdom ('What hast thou done unto us?', the heathen king rebuking the patriarch, the just reproof for the fault, as Pharaoh, 12:18, the humbling of Abraham, reproved by a pagan); Abraham's deception 'brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin' (the king and his people brought near to a great sin, adultery, unwittingly, and to the judgment it would have incurred, the deception having endangered the whole kingdom, the gravity of the consequence of Abraham's fault); 'thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done' (Abimelech reproaching Abraham for acting wrongly, doing what ought not to be done, the just complaint against the deception, the patriarch, who should have been a witness of truth, reproved by a heathen for his fault, the shame of Abraham's relapse, cf. 12:18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Then Abimelech called Abraham, and said unto him, What hast thou done unto us?... thou hast done deeds unto me that ought not to be done
Abimelech's rebuke of Abraham
the heathen king justly reproving the patriarch for the deception
the shame of Abraham's relapse, reproved by a pagan (12:18)

schools.summary: Abimelech asks Abraham's reason: 'and Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?' — the king asking what Abraham had seen or thought (what reason or fear) that led him to the deception (the inquiry into the motive, the king pressing Abraham to explain his fault, the demand for an account of his conduct); the king asking what Abraham 'sawest' (perceived, feared, or had in view) that prompted the deception (the inquiry into Abraham's reasoning, the king seeking to understand the patriarch's motive); 'that thou hast done this thing' (Abimelech demanding an explanation of Abraham's act, the call for the patriarch to give account, the question that drew out Abraham's reasoning, vv.11-13 — his fear, v.11, the half-truth, v.12, and his practice, v.13, the king's just inquiry) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And Abimelech said unto Abraham, What sawest thou, that thou hast done this thing?
Abimelech asking Abraham's reason for the deception
the inquiry into the patriarch's motive
the demand for an account, drawing out Abraham's reasoning (vv.11-13)

schools.summary: Abraham's reason - the fear of God not in the place: 'and Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife's sake' — Abraham explaining his deception: he had supposed that there was no fear of God in Gerar (among the heathen Philistines), and so no restraint on wickedness (the patriarch's reasoning that, in a godless place, his life would be in danger for his wife's sake); Abraham's supposition, in part, a misjudgment (he having presumed the worst of the place, yet there being found more fear of God in Abimelech, vv.5-6, than Abraham had expected, the patriarch's fear, born of his prejudgment, leading him into the fault, the warning against presuming the worst and acting on fear rather than faith, yet the fear of God indeed lacking enough that such things were a real danger); 'they will slay me for my wife's sake' (Abraham fearing that the men of Gerar, lacking the fear of God, would kill him to take Sarah, as he had feared in Egypt, 12:12, the fear of man, Proverbs 29:25, driving the deception, the weakness of faith, looking to the danger rather than trusting God's protection, the same fear that had led to the first fault) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Abraham said, Because I thought, Surely the fear of God is not in this place; and they will slay me for my wife's sake
Abraham's reason - his fear that there was no fear of God in Gerar
the misjudgment of the place, more fear of God found than expected (vv.5-6)
the fear of man (Proverbs 29:25) driving the deception, the weakness of faith

schools.summary: Abraham's defense - Sarah his half-sister: 'and yet indeed she is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife' — Abraham defending his saying (v.2) as a half-truth: Sarah indeed his half-sister (the daughter of his father Terah by another mother, or some taking her his niece, called 'sister' in the loose usage of kindred, cf. 11:29, the relation variously reckoned, the saying 'she is my sister' thus literally true, in a sense, the equivocation resting on a real kinship); Abraham pleading the literal truth of his words — Sarah his sister (half-sister or near kin, the saying not a direct lie but a suppression of the fuller truth, that she was also his wife, the equivocation, while resting on a fact, meant to deceive, the fault in the concealment and the intent, not in a bare falsehood); 'she became my wife' (the truth Abraham concealed that Sarah, his sister, had become his wife, the marriage of near kin, before the later law forbidding such, Leviticus 18:9, the fact suppressed, the half-truth concealing the whole, the fault of the deception, however the kinship be reckoned) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And yet indeed she is my sister; she is the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife
Abraham's defense - Sarah his half-sister
the saying literally true (a real kinship), yet the whole truth concealed
the fault in the deceiving intent, not a bare falsehood

schools.summary: Abraham's long-standing practice: 'and it came to pass, when God caused me to wander from my father's house, that I said unto her, This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me; at every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother' — Abraham referring to his call and pilgrimage (12:1, the wandering, sojourning life of the patriarch, a stranger in the land, from the beginning of his wandering he having made this arrangement with Sarah, the long-standing practice begun at his call); Abraham having said to Sarah, 'This is thy kindness which thou shalt shew unto me' (he asking her, as a kindness, to support the arrangement, the agreement between them, made at the outset of their wandering, the practice settled long before); 'at every place... say of me, He is my brother' (Abraham's standing policy, to call himself Sarah's brother wherever they went, the deception not a single act but a settled practice, the patriarch's habitual reliance on this stratagem, born of fear, the fault, long-continued, exposed, the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful, leaning on his own contrivance through the years) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, when God caused me to wander from my father's house... at every place whither we shall come, say of me, He is my brother
Abraham's confession of his long-standing practice
the deception a settled policy, born of fear, begun at his call (12:1)
the weakness of faith leaning on his own contrivance through the years

schools.summary: Abimelech restores Sarah and gives gifts: 'and Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife' — the king giving Abraham generous gifts (flocks, herds, servants, the conciliation of the patriarch, and perhaps amends for the wrong of taking Sarah, the honoring of Abraham, whom God had warned the king to regard, v.7, the king's liberality); Abimelech 'restored him Sarah his wife' (the king returning Sarah, untouched, v.6, to Abraham, the deliverance of Sarah, by God's protecting providence, the promise, the seed through Sarah, preserved, the restoring of the wife to her husband, as God had commanded, v.7); Abimelech's restoring Sarah and enriching Abraham showing God's overruling providence (Sarah preserved and Abraham honored, despite the patriarch's fault, the deliverance resting on God's faithfulness, not Abraham's wit, the LORD bringing good even out of the fault, as in Egypt, 12:16, 20, the protection of the covenant-promise) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abimelech took sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and womenservants, and gave them unto Abraham, and restored him Sarah his wife
Abimelech restoring Sarah and giving gifts to Abraham
Sarah returned untouched, the promise preserved (v.6)
God's overruling providence, Sarah preserved and Abraham honored despite his fault

schools.summary: Abimelech invites Abraham to dwell: 'and Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee' — the king offering Abraham freedom to dwell anywhere in his land (the generous, conciliatory invitation, the honoring of the patriarch, whom God had warned the king to regard, v.7, the welcome to settle in Gerar); Abimelech freely offering his land to Abraham (a mark of the king's good will and his fear of God, after the warning, the contrast with Pharaoh, who sent Abraham away, 12:20, the generous dealing of Abimelech); 'dwell where it pleaseth thee' (the king giving Abraham leave to settle where he chose, the welcome to the patriarch, the providence of God giving Abraham favor in the land, the sojourner welcomed, the covenant-man honored among the Philistines, cf. 21:22-34, the later covenant with Abimelech) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Abimelech said, Behold, my land is before thee: dwell where it pleaseth thee
Abimelech inviting Abraham to dwell in his land
the generous, conciliatory welcome of the patriarch
the providence of God giving Abraham favor among the Philistines

schools.summary: The gift to Sarah and the covering of the eyes: 'and unto Sarah he said, Behold, I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver: behold, he is to thee a covering of the eyes, unto all that are with thee, and with all other: thus she was reproved' — Abimelech giving Abraham (ironically called Sarah's 'brother,' as she had said) a thousand pieces (shekels) of silver (a large sum, the gift to Abraham, and so to Sarah, as amends and vindication, the honoring of Sarah and her husband); 'he (the gift, or Abraham) is to thee a covering of the eyes' variously understood: (a) the thousand pieces of silver a vindication, a 'covering of the eyes,' a public token of Sarah's innocence — to cover (stop) the eyes and mouths of any who might reproach her, that none should look on her as one wronged or guilty, the open clearing of her honor; (b) a veil to conceal her beauty — that she should go veiled, as a married woman, that her beauty not again expose her; (c) the husband (Abraham) is to her a 'covering of the eyes,' her protector and guardian; the phrase genuinely difficult, the dominant sense taking the gift as a public vindication of Sarah's honor; 'thus she was reproved' (or 'she was righted/vindicated') the Hebrew variously rendered, either that Sarah was reproved (gently rebuked for her part in the deception, v.5), or that she was vindicated/righted (her honor cleared by the public gift), whether a reproof of Sarah or her vindication, the affair openly set right — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'he is to thee a covering of the eyes' (and 'thus she was reproved') mean?

sides.position: A vindication/covering of the eyes - the gift a public token of Sarah's innocence, to stop the reproach of others; 'thus she was righted/vindicated' (the dominant reading)
A veil to conceal her beauty (that she go veiled as a married woman); or the husband as her protector; and 'thus she was reproved' = gently rebuked for her part

themes: And unto Sarah he said, Behold, I have given thy brother a thousand pieces of silver: behold, he is to thee a covering of the eyes... thus she was reproved
the gift to Sarah and the difficult 'covering of the eyes' (the crux of the phrase)
a public vindication of Sarah's honor vs a veil to conceal her beauty vs the husband as protector
'thus she was reproved' - a gentle reproof or her being righted

schools.summary: Abraham's prayer heals Abimelech's house: 'so Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bare children' — the patriarch, as the prophet (v.7), interceding for Abimelech (his prayer fulfilling God's word, v.7, 'he shall pray for thee,' the intercession of the friend of God, the prophet's prayer for the king who had wronged him, the returning of good for the rebuke); 'God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants' (God, in answer to Abraham's prayer, healing Abimelech and the women of his house of the affliction, the closing of the wombs, v.18, perhaps also a disease on the king, the power of the prophet's intercession, God's mercy restoring the household); 'they bare children' (the women, healed, again bearing children, the wombs, closed by God, v.18, now opened, the removal of the judgment, the fruit of Abraham's prayer, the restoration of fruitfulness to the house, the power of the intercession of the righteous, James 5:16) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: So Abraham prayed unto God: and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his maidservants; and they bare children
Abraham's prayer healing Abimelech's house
the prophet's intercession (v.7) prevailing for the king who had wronged him
the power of the righteous man's prayer (James 5:16)

schools.summary: The LORD had closed the wombs for Sarah's sake: 'for the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah Abraham's wife' — God having visited the household with barrenness (closing the wombs of the women, and perhaps a disease on the king, vv.6, 17, the judgment, a restraint, v.6, that both prevented the sin and warned the king, the affliction that signaled God's displeasure and protected Sarah); the closing of the wombs (and the affliction) the means by which God restrained Abimelech from touching Sarah (v.6) and warned him (the providence that protected Sarah's honor, the affliction lifted by Abraham's prayer, v.17); 'because of Sarah Abraham's wife' (the affliction coming because of the taking of Sarah, Abraham's wife, God's regard to Sarah and the marriage bond, the LORD's protection of the matriarch, and the promise of the seed through her, 17:19; 18:10, by afflicting the house that had taken her, the judgment removed when Sarah was restored and Abraham prayed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: For the LORD had fast closed up all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah Abraham's wife
that the LORD had closed the wombs of Abimelech's house for Sarah's sake
the affliction the means of restraint and warning, protecting Sarah's honor
God's protection of the matriarch and the promise of the seed (17:19; 18:10)
